title: Formation of bound composite vortices of a singly-quantized $^1$S$_0$ vortex and half-quantized $^3$P$_2$ vortices in the $^1$S$_0$-$^3$P$_2$ coexisting phase in neutron stars
authors: Tatsuhiro Hattori, Muneto Nitta, Kazuyuki Sekizawa
abstract: Pulsar glitches are believed to originate from the dynamics of quantized vortices in the neutron superfluid interior. The outer core of a neutron star hosts a $^3\text{P}_2$ spin-triplet superfluid, whose half-integer quantum vortices (HQVs) are qualitatively different from the $^1\text{S}_0$ singly quantized vortices (SQVs) in the inner crust. It has recently been proposed that the coupling between these two vortex species gives rise to a large-scale vortex network, providing a candidate mechanism for the diversity of observed pulsar glitch phenomena. Using the Gross--Pitaevskii equations for the $^1\text{S}_0$ and $^3\text{P}_2$ condensates, we perform two-dimensional simulations of one SQV and two HQVs in a coexistence phase near the crust-core boundary, varying the density--density and Josephson coupling constants. We find that the Josephson term, arising from the relative phase between the two condensates, induces a strong attractive interaction between the two HQVs and the SQV, which dominates over the density--density coupling. When pinning potentials are applied to the HQVs and the SQV at spatially separated locations, this attraction is found to be sufficiently strong to drive vortex depinning. These results suggest that two HQVs and one SQV can form a tightly bound composite vortex at the crust-core boundary, with implications for the glitch mechanism in neutron stars.
summary: The paper shows that the phase-sensitive Josephson coupling between singlet and triplet neutron condensates creates a strong attraction between different types of quantized vortices. This interaction can overcome pinning forces to form bound composite vortex structures, providing a potential microscopic mechanism for pulsar glitches.
main_problem: Investigating the microscopic interaction and structural dynamics between singly-quantized (1S0) and half-quantized (3P2) neutron vortices in the coexistence phase of neutron star interiors.
main_result: The Josephson coupling between the two condensates induces a strong attractive interaction that drives vortex depinning and leads to the formation of a bound 'vortex molecule' structure (HQV-SQV-HQV).
method: Two-dimensional numerical simulations using the Gross-Pitaevskii equations and the imaginary-time evolution method to find ground-state configurations.
model_or_system: A multi-component system consisting of a spin-singlet (1S0) Bose-Einstein Condensate and a spin-2 (3P2) spinor Bose-Einstein Condensate, representing the crust-core boundary of a neutron star.
key_observables: Vortex spatial configuration, Josephson energy density, inter-vortex distance, and phase winding numbers.
important_parameters: Density-density coupling constant (zeta1), Josephson/pair-exchange coupling constant (zeta2), and pinning potentials.
main_assumptions: The study uses the Gross-Pitaevskii framework, which assumes a zero-temperature limit and may not be fully accurate near the transition temperature.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the initial configuration with pinned vortices; Figure 6 shows the ground state at low coupling where vortices remain trapped.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the pulsar glitch problem, describes the GP model for the 1S0-3P2 coexistence phase, details the numerical simulation protocol, presents results on vortex attraction and bound state formation, and discusses astrophysical implications.
why_interesting: The study demonstrates a direct analogy between neutron star vortex dynamics and the Josephson coupling observed in experimental bilayer and spinor BEC systems, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics in ultracold gases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Josephson term density R d3r ∑2 m=−2  (−1)mσ2ψ∗2 m +(−1)mσ∗2ψ2 m  as a function of the pair-exchange coupling constant ζ2 for three values of the density–density coupling constant ζ1, as indicated in the legend. The system consists of one SQV in the 1S0 condensate and two HQVs in the 3P2 condensate.

FIG. 2: Inter-vortex distance dHQV between the two HQVs in the 3P2 condensate as a function of the pair-exchange coupling constant ζ2 for three values of the density–density coupling constant ζ1, as indicated in the legend. The HQV positions are identified from the phase singularities of Ψ20(r) as described in Sec. II C. The system configuration and imaginary-time evolution procedure are the same as in Fig. 1.

FIG. 3: Color maps of the ground-state configuration obtained by imaginary-time evolution without pinning potentials for both condensates and ζ1 = 0, ˜ζ2 = 0.010(a) Density |Ψ20|2 of the 3P2 condensate, showing the two HQV cores as density-depleted regions. (b) Phase arg[Ψ20] of the 3P2 condensate, displaying the 2π phase winding around each HQV core. (c) Density |σ|2 of the 1S0 condensate, showing the SQV core as a single density-depleted region. (d) Phase arg[σ] of the 1S0 condensate, displaying the 2π phase winding around the SQV core. (e) Spatial distribution of the Josephson energy density σ2 tr(A∗2)+σ∗2 tr(A2), reflecting the relative phase between the two condensates. All panels...

FIG. 4: Color maps of the ground-state configuration obtained by imaginary-time evolution without pinning potentials for both condensates and ζ1 = 0, ˜ζ2 = 0.150. (a) Density |Ψ20|2 of the 3P2 condensate, showing the two HQV cores as density-depleted regions. (b) Phase arg[Ψ20] of the 3P2 condensate, displaying the 2π phase winding around each HQV core. (c) Density |σ|2 of the 1S0 condensate, showing the SQV core as a single density-depleted region. (d) Phase arg[σ] of the 1S0 condensate, displaying the 2π phase winding around the SQV core. (e) Spatial distribution of the Josephson energy density σ2 tr(A∗2)+σ∗2 tr(A2), reflecting the relative phase between the two condensates. All panels...

FIG. 6: Josephson term density R d3r ∑2 m=−2  (−1)mσ2ψ∗2 m +(−1)mσ∗2ψ2 m  as a function of the pair-exchange coupling constant ζ2 for three values of the pinning site distances of HQV, as indicated in the legend. Here, ζ1 = 0. The system consists of one SQV in the 1S0 condensate and two HQVs in the 3P2 condensate, with singlet and triplet pinning position.
